module: Visiting the Messenger and the Graves
title: Women Visiting the Graves and the Prophet's Grave
short_rule: Hanafis recommend women visit the Prophet's grave without dislike, while Hanbalis permit women to visit general graves if done infrequently.
details: The Hanafi school holds the sound position that women visiting the Prophet's grave is recommended (Mustahab) and not disliked. The Hanbali school notes disagreement regarding women visiting graves generally, but deems it acceptable (Mubah) provided they do not do so frequently.
actionable_steps: If following the Hanafi school, women are encouraged to partake in the blessings of visiting the Prophet's grave.
If following the Hanbali school, women may visit graves but should limit the frequency of their visits.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on 'Aisha visiting her brother's grave and the Prophet's general command: 'Visit them for they remind of death.'
spiritual_wisdom: Visiting graves reminds the believer of the afterlife and human mortality, while the specific visit to the Prophet yields immense spiritual blessings for all believers.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn Qudamah

module: Visiting the Messenger and the Graves
title: Donating the Reward of Deeds to the Deceased
short_rule: Hanafis and Hanbalis allow the transfer of reward from physical, financial, and Qur'anic worship to the deceased.
details: The Hanafi and Hanbali schools agree that a believer can designate the reward of voluntary good deeds (such as prayer, fasting, hajj, charity, and reciting the Qur'an) to the deceased, and that the reward reaches them. The Hanbali text clarifies that while reciting Qur'an for the dead is permitted (also the popular Shafi'i opinion), gathering specifically to recite the Qur'an for them is an innovation.
actionable_steps: Perform voluntary acts of worship such as charity, fasting, or Qur'an recitation.
Designate the reward of these actions to your deceased loved ones so it benefits them in the Afterlife.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on the Prophet's instruction allowing a man to donate charity on behalf of his parents, and scholarly extension to bodily worship like fasting and recitation.
spiritual_wisdom: Maintains the spiritual bond between the living and the dead, manifesting the unity, intercession, and mutual mercy of the Muslim community across the boundary of death.
author: Al-Shurunbulali, Ibn Qudamah

module: Visiting the Messenger and the Graves
title: Raising Hands Upon First Seeing the Ka'ba
short_rule: Hanafis consider it disliked to raise the hands when supplicating upon first seeing the Ka'ba.
details: While making supplication (du'a) upon first seeing the Sacred House is highly recommended because it is a moment of accepted prayers, the Hanafi school (as narrated by Imam Tahawi from Abu Hanifa, Abu Yusuf, and Muhammad) deems it disliked (Makruh) to physically raise one's hands during this specific initial supplication.
actionable_steps: Make earnest supplication upon first laying eyes on the Ka'ba, as it is a time of accepted prayers.
Keep your hands down while making this initial supplication if following the strict Hanafi etiquette.
quran_hadith_evidence: Derived from specific transmitted etiquettes regarding the physical demeanor of the pilgrim entering the Sacred Precinct.
spiritual_wisdom: Restricting the physical gesture of raising hands emphasizes a state of silent, overwhelming awe upon first encountering the majesty of the Sacred House.
author: Al-Shurunbulali

module: Visiting the Messenger and the Graves
title: Superiority of Makka versus Madina
short_rule: Hanafis deem Makka generally superior to Madina, except for the specific ground containing the Prophet's blessed body.
details: The Hanafi school maintains that the city of Makka is fundamentally better than Madina. However, they make a profound exception for the exact plot of earth that is honored by physically touching the blessed body of the Prophet Muhammad; this specific ground is considered superior to the rest of creation, including the Ka'ba and the Divine Throne.
actionable_steps: Hold immense reverence for both sacred cities.
Recognize Makka's general superiority while acknowledging the absolute unmatched sanctity of the Prophet's resting place.
quran_hadith_evidence: Based on theological veneration of the Prophet and the spiritual weight of his physical presence.
spiritual_wisdom: Balances the objective sacredness of the Divine House (Ka'ba) with the supreme honor bestowed upon the Best of Creation, Prophet Muhammad.
author: Al-Shurunbulali

module: Visiting the Messenger and the Graves
title: Direction of Facing While Greeting the Prophet
short_rule: Hanafis recommend facing the Prophet's grave with one's back to the Qibla when offering greetings.
details: According to the Hanafi text, it is recommended (Mustahab) when visiting the Prophet's grave to stand facing his blessed face, which means the visitor will have their back toward the Qibla, standing at a respectful distance of about two meters.
actionable_steps: When visiting the Prophet's grave, stand respectfully facing his resting place.
Turn your back to the Qibla while offering the salutations to him.
quran_hadith_evidence: Established by the continuous practice and etiquette of the Companions and early generations when visiting the Rawda.
spiritual_wisdom: Directing oneself entirely toward the Prophet during the greeting reflects utmost respect, undivided attention, and recognition of his status as the primary intermediary of guidance.
author: Al-Shurunbulali
